originalTitle: Australia news live: new Labor president Kate Ellis warns party against ‘turning hurt into anger’; Hanson accuses media of twisting her words on DV
content: Escalating conflict in the Middle East reignites oil price debate Brent crude prices will need to rise as high as $US150 a barrel if the worsening Middle East conflict restricts traffic through the strait of Hormuz for another 10 weeks, CBA experts warn. The global oil benchmark climbed to over $US96 a barrel this morning after Houthi rebels claimed responsibility for attacks on two tankers in the Red Sea. Crude prices are now up by a third this month and may have further to go. John Oh, an energy economist at CBA, said Saudi Arabian oil would for the most part find other ways to get to the market even were the Red Sea shipping routes blocked. But he warned that the latest disruptions coincided with already low global inventories following months of additional drawdowns. CBA experts predict the hostilities to extend for a further two months, which should avoid the worst-case scenario outlined above, and the bank’s economists forecast Brent crude trading between $US70 and $US100 a barrel in the second half of this year. Oh: Whilst we see upside risks for oil, it’s worth noting that markets have been optimistic about a swift return to prewar levels given pipeline bypasses, reduced Chinese oil imports and non-OPEC+ supply growth have alleviated oil market’s dependence on oil tanker traffic through the strait since the war began. The ALP national conference is under way, with Kate Ellis taking the stage as the incoming president of the organisational wing. She followed a welcome to country by Uncle Mickey. Ellis says her predecessor, Wayne Swan, is a “giant of the movement”, which receives a big round of applause from the audience. She notes it’s the first time in history Labor has had a president and two vice-presidents who are more women, and offers a pointed remark: While others bicker about if and how and when women deserve representation within our political structures, we’re just getting on with the job. She adds that she doesn’t stand here because she misses the limelight. “I really didn’t,” she chuckles, but that there’s “too much at stake for anyone to sit down on the sidelines”. We face a time when there is a massive political realignment happening, both globally and here at home ... There are people hurting. Some of them are really angry about it, and at this conference, we show them that rather than turning our back on those people or turning their hurt into more anger and division, or grandstanding and chasing cheap headlines, will do what Labor does best. We will work to make the real decisions that improve the lives of the Australian people. ALP national conference kicks off in Adelaide Hello from the ALP national conference 2026! I’m in Adelaide and will be bringing you all the action from the debates, announcements, speeches and fights. Here’s a bit of a rundown on what’s happening: First the former Rudd/Gillard minister, Kate Ellis, will officially become the new president of Labor’s organisational wing. She’ll say a few words, before the South Australian Labor premier, Peter Malinauskas, takes the stage. Then the spotlight will be on Anthony Albanese. We’ve only seen snippets of his speech – including his announcement of the extension of paid prac placements for allied health services. The national platform, which outlines Labor’s whole agenda, will be debated throughout the conference, and the first few hours of today will be spent debating the economic agenda. That includes some sticking points such as gas tax policy, fuel tax credits and AI policy. We’ll also be bringing you the action from some of the so-called fringe events that happen on the sidelines of the conference and any protest action outside. Helen Haines says new payments for health students must be delivered ‘as soon as possible’ The independent federal member for Indi, Dr Helen Haines, has joined peak bodies in welcoming the news that the federal government will extend its paid practical placements scheme to allied health students. It follows continued lobbying from the health workforce, the Greens and a coalition of independents, including Haines. In June, Haines joined independent senator David Pocock to present a petition to the parliament signed by more than 43,000 people calling for placements to be made available to allied health and medical students experiencing “placement poverty”. Haines said: This is fantastic news for allied health students across Australia and alleviate enormous pressure they face when trying to juggle study and their day-to-day bills. At a time of urgent health workforce shortages, particularly in rural and regional Australia, we cannot afford to have financial pressures preventing students from completing health degrees. I’m keen to see the detail and to ensure these payments are delivered as soon as possible. Students who are struggling with placement poverty need support now. Read more about the announcement and what the scheme does here: Pauline Hanson claims her highly criticised comments on domestic violence were ‘twisted by the media’ Pauline Hanson is continuing to be criticised by domestic violence experts for comments she made about the scourge, but the One Nation leader has put the blame on the media. After comments she made in public interviews came under fire, Hanson claimed her words had been “twisted by the media” and denied she had downplayed domestic violence. She told 4BC radio yesterday it was “wrong but it’s also a two-way street”. I’m not saying that it’s just men, women can lose their cool as well. Society is under so much pressure … Everyone is under so much pressure and it’s really hard … Domestic violence is a scourge but look … I’ve been through it myself in my life. And I just feel if you’re not in a house where you feel safe … then you shouldn’t put up with it. If these women are getting abused by men out there, then don’t put up with it. Yesterday, after the comments were criticised, she posted a transcript of those quotes online, writing: “Here’s
chineseTitle: 澳大利亚新闻直播：新任工党主席凯特·埃利斯警告该党不要“将伤害转化为愤怒”；汉森指责媒体歪曲她在 DV 中的言论
chineseContent: 中东冲突升级重新引发油价争论 CBA 专家警告称，如果不断恶化的中东冲突导致霍尔木兹海峡交通再持续 10 周，布伦特原油价格将需要上涨至每桶 150 美元。在胡塞叛军声称对红海两艘油轮袭击事件负责后，今天上午全球石油基准价格攀升至每桶 96 美元以上。本月原油价格现已上涨三分之一，并且可能还会进一步上涨。 CBA能源经济学家John Oh表示，即使红海航线被封锁，沙特阿拉伯的石油在很大程度上也会找到其他方式进入市场。但他警告说，最近的供应中断恰逢全球库存在数月的进一步缩减之后已经很低。 CBA专家预测，敌对行动将再持续两个月，这应该可以避免上述最坏情况的发生，该行经济学家预测今年下半年布伦特原油价格将在每桶70美元至100美元之间。 Oh：虽然我们看到石油存在上行风险，但值得注意的是，鉴于自战争开始以来管道绕道、中国石油进口减少和非欧佩克+供应增长减轻了石油市场对海峡油轮运输的依赖，市场对石油迅速恢复到战前水平持乐观态度。工党全国会议正在进行中，凯特·埃利斯 (Kate Ellis) 作为即将上任的组织部门主席上台。米奇叔叔欢迎她来到这个国家。埃利斯称她的前任韦恩·斯旺是“这场运动的巨人”，赢得了观众的热烈掌声。她指出，这是工党历史上第一次出现一位女性较多的总统和两位副总统，并提出了尖锐的评论：
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于全球新闻信号，适合用于观察外部环境、政策变化、地缘风险和市场情绪。
